prompts.baseline:
Gary Marcus posted on X:

"Anyone expecting the current paradigm to be close to AGI is delusional."

How likely is it that AGI will be reached primarily through scaling and refining large language model architectures (by any lab or company), rather than through paradigm shifts to fundamentally different approaches, before 2035? Weigh the relevant considerations and respond with a single best-estimate probability.

rows.answer:
Assuming **AGI** means a system that can autonomously perform or learn most cognitive work at roughly strong-human level across domains, and assuming “LLM architecture” includes **multimodal transformer/foundation-model descendants with tool use, memory, RL/post-training, synthetic data, inference-time search, and agent scaffolding**, my best estimate is:

**35%**

Why not lower: scaling has repeatedly produced unexpectedly broad capabilities; current foundation models are already the only demonstrated path with general language, coding, reasoning, and tool-use abilities; and any AGI before 2035 is more likely to grow out of the dominant, heavily funded paradigm than from a still-unproven alternative.

Why not higher: “AGI” requires much more reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, self-correction, continual learning, and robust autonomy than today’s systems show. Data/compute bottlenecks, diminishing returns, hallucination/reliability problems, and the possibility that new algorithmic ideas—not just refined LLMs—are needed all materially reduce the odds.

So my single best-estimate probability that **AGI is reached before 2035 primarily via scaled/refined LLM-style architectures rather than a fundamentally different paradigm** is **35%**.

rows.answer:
Assuming “AGI” means a system that can robustly perform and learn across most economically useful cognitive tasks at roughly human level, and counting multimodal transformer/foundation-model descendants with tools, memory, retrieval, RL/post-training, and test-time reasoning as “LLM refinement,” my best estimate is:

**35%**

Why not lower: scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected; language/code-trained models may be a surprisingly general substrate; tool use, synthetic data, verifiers, memory, and agent scaffolding could turn today’s brittle models into much more reliable systems; and if AGI arrives before 2035, the dominant research path is very likely to be foundation-model-centered.

Why not higher: current systems still struggle with robust long-horizon planning, grounded causal understanding, continual learning, reliability under distribution shift, and autonomous agency. Scaling returns may flatten due to data, compute, energy, or architectural limits. It is also plausible that real AGI needs a more fundamental shift—e.g. new learning objectives, world-model architectures, neurosymbolic systems, embodied interaction, or substantially different cognitive architectures.

So I would not call LLM-scaling optimism “delusional,” but I also would not make it the majority forecast. **Single best-estimate probability: 35%.**

rows.answer:
Interpreting “AGI” as robust human-level-or-better performance across most cognitive tasks, and “LLM paradigm” broadly as transformer-like foundation models scaled/refined with multimodality, tools, memory, RL/post-training, synthetic data, agents, and inference-time reasoning:

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Why not higher: current LLMs still show major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounded understanding, and autonomous task execution. Scaling may face data, compute, energy, and diminishing-returns constraints, and benchmark gains may overstate real generality.

Why not much lower: the paradigm has repeatedly absorbed objections through scale, post-training, tool use, multimodality, code execution, retrieval, and agent scaffolding. It is plausible that what looks like “missing reasoning” is partly an engineering, data, and inference-time-compute problem rather than evidence that a wholly new paradigm is required.

So my view is: **LLM-centered AGI before 2035 is a serious possibility but not the default outcome — about 35%.**

rows.answer:
Under a fairly demanding definition of AGI—an AI system that can autonomously perform most cognitive tasks at roughly skilled-human level across domains, learn new tasks with little hand-holding, and operate reliably over long horizons—my best estimate is:

**40%**

That is, I’d put about a **40% probability** on AGI arriving before 2035 primarily through continued scaling/refinement of LLM-style foundation models rather than a clearly new paradigm.

My rough reasoning:

- **Reasons the probability is substantial:** LLMs have already shown surprising generality; scaling, multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, synthetic data, RL, and test-time search may plausibly address many current weaknesses without abandoning the paradigm. There is also enormous economic pressure and capital pushing this path forward.
- **Reasons it is not higher:** Current systems still struggle with robust causal reasoning, long-horizon autonomy, grounded world models, continual learning, reliability, and compounding-error control. It is plausible that solving these requires something more like explicit planning/world-model architectures, neuro-symbolic systems, embodied learning, or other approaches that would count as a meaningful paradigm shift.
- **Timeline uncertainty:** Before 2035 is close enough that compute, data, energy, regulation, and deployment bottlenecks matter. Even if LLM-like systems are on the right track, nine years may not be enough for full AGI by a demanding standard.

A rough decomposition would be: maybe **~55%** chance of AGI before 2035 under this definition, and **~70%** chance that, conditional on that happening, the winning system is still primarily an LLM/foundation-model lineage. Multiplying gives about **40%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I would not call it “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome.

By “primarily through scaling and refining LLM architectures,” I’m counting systems where transformer/foundation-model-style LLMs remain the central cognitive engine, even if enhanced with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, agents, synthetic data, reinforcement learning, inference-time search, and better post-training. I’m excluding outcomes where AGI mainly comes from a fundamentally different paradigm such as a new symbolic/neurosymbolic architecture, radically different world-model RL, brain-inspired systems, or something not recognizably descended from today’s LLM/foundation-model approach.

Reasons the probability is substantial:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- LLM-like systems already show nontrivial generality across language, coding, math, vision, tool use, and planning-like tasks.
- The dominant labs, capital, hardware, and talent are overwhelmingly pushing the foundation-model paradigm.
- “Refinements” such as multimodal training, agent scaffolding, memory, self-play/synthetic data, and inference-time compute could plausibly close many current gaps without a clean paradigm break.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is more likely to come from the currently compounding paradigm than from an unproven alternative with less infrastructure.

Reasons the probability is far from overwhelming:

- Current systems remain brittle in reliability, long-horizon autonomy, causal reasoning, continual learning, and robust real-world grounding.
- Scaling may face compute, data, energy, economics, or diminishing-returns constraints.
- Some deficits may not be patchable by “more model + better post-training” and may require deeper changes in learning, planning, memory, or world modeling.
- “AGI” is a high bar if it means robust human-level competence across unfamiliar domains, not merely impressive benchmark performance or economically useful automation.
- The eventual successful system may blend LLMs with other methods enough that calling it “primarily LLM scaling” becomes misleading.

My decomposition would be roughly: a meaningful chance of AGI before 2035, conditional on that a fairly high chance the winning path is still foundation-model/LLM-centered, but with enough uncertainty about both AGI timelines and architectural sufficiency to keep the final estimate well below 50%.

**Single best estimate: 35%.**

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’m interpreting the question as: before 2035, a broadly human-level general AI is achieved, and the decisive technical path is still recognizably the LLM/foundation-model path—scaled transformers or close descendants, multimodality, RL/post-training, synthetic data, tool use, memory, agents, and test-time reasoning—rather than a fundamentally different cognitive architecture.

Why that probability is not tiny:

- LLM scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- The architecture is flexible: language, code, vision, tool use, planning traces, and interactive data can all be folded into the same general training paradigm.
- Many apparent weaknesses—math, coding, long context, tool use, self-correction—have improved through engineering and post-training rather than paradigm replacement.
- If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to come from the massively funded, already-working foundation-model ecosystem than from a clean-sheet alternative.

Why I keep it below even odds:

- Current LLM-style systems still show brittleness, hallucination, weak causal grounding, poor long-horizon autonomy, and unreliable self-monitoring.
- “AGI” requires robust generalization under novelty, not just benchmark success or fluent task performance.
- Data, compute, energy, and deployment bottlenecks may make continued scaling less fruitful.
- The missing pieces may require deeper changes: persistent world models, continual learning, active experimentation, formal reasoning, embodiment, or architectures not well described as refined LLMs.

So I would not call belief in the LLM path “delusional,” but I also would not treat AGI-by-scaling as the default outcome. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’d interpret the question as: *by 2035, a system broadly deserving the label AGI—robustly capable across most economically and intellectually important cognitive tasks—will be achieved primarily by scaling/refining transformer-like, LLM-centered architectures, including multimodality, tool use, memory, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and agent scaffolding, rather than requiring a substantially new paradigm.*

Why this is plausible:

- **Scaling has worked surprisingly well so far.** Larger models, better data, better post-training, multimodal inputs, and tool use have repeatedly converted “LLMs can’t do X” into “LLMs can often do X.”
- **The current paradigm is extensible.** Retrieval, code execution, verifiers, long-context memory, self-play, test-time search, synthetic data, and agent frameworks can be seen as refinements rather than paradigm shifts.
- **Economic incentives are enormous.** Multiple labs and states have strong motivation to push compute, data, and algorithmic efficiency hard through 2035.
- **Human cognition may not require an entirely different recipe.** It is possible that sufficiently large predictive/world-modeling systems plus interaction, tools, and RL-like feedback can approximate the needed generality.

Why I don’t put it higher:

- **Current systems still have deep reliability problems.** Hallucination, brittle reasoning, poor long-horizon autonomy, weak causal understanding, and inconsistent self-correction may not vanish with scale alone.
- **Data and compute scaling may hit diminishing returns.** High-quality human-generated data is finite, and further scaling may become economically or physically constrained.
- **AGI may require stronger forms of learning and agency.** Continual learning, grounded world models, causal reasoning, planning, and autonomous experimentation may need architectures or training regimes that are more than “LLM refinement.”
- **Benchmarks can mislead.** Systems can look broadly intelligent while still failing in open-ended, adversarial, or real-world settings.

So I would not call belief in LLM-centered AGI by 2035 “delusional”; it is a serious possibility. But I also think it is far from assured. My single best estimate is therefore:

**35%**.

**Best-estimate probability: 30%.**

Interpreting the event as: *before 2035, some lab achieves broadly human-level, economically useful general intelligence, and the main technical lineage is recognizably scaled/refined LLM-style systems—multimodal transformers, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL/post-training, test-time reasoning—rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.*

- Scaling has worked surprisingly well so far across language, code, math, multimodal tasks, and tool use.
- Frontier labs have enormous incentives, capital, talent, and compute.
- LLM-based systems are becoming agentic platforms, not just chatbots: they can call tools, write code, use search, critique outputs, and operate over long contexts.

- Scaling may face bottlenecks: data quality, compute cost, energy, latency, and diminishing returns.
- “AGI” may require stronger forms of continual learning, embodied/world-model reasoning, or agency than LLM refinement can deliver.
- A future system may still use LLMs but owe the decisive advance to a different paradigm—e.g. model-based RL, neuro-symbolic reasoning, new memory architectures, or self-improving agents.

So I’d say Gary Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong, but so is confident AGI-soon optimism. My single best estimate is **about a one-in-three chance: 30%**.

**35% likelihood that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily through scaled/refined LLM-style architectures.**

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’m interpreting this as an **unconditional** forecast: by 2035, AGI is actually achieved, and the decisive route is mainly an evolved LLM/foundation-model paradigm—larger/more efficient transformer-like models, multimodality, RL/post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, verifiers, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, etc.—rather than a fundamentally new symbolic, cognitive-architecture, brain-inspired, or embodied-learning paradigm where LLMs are peripheral.

Why it’s plausible:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- LLMs are a flexible substrate: language, code, tool use, planning traces, and multimodal data can all be absorbed into the same general training framework.
- Many apparent weaknesses—hallucination, weak long-horizon planning, poor reliability—may be reduced by better training, retrieval, external memory, verification, test-time compute, and agentic systems rather than requiring a total paradigm break.
- The leading labs, capital, infrastructure, and talent are overwhelmingly concentrated on foundation-model descendants, so if AGI arrives soon, this is the most likely path.

Why I don’t put it higher:

- Current systems still have serious gaps in causal reasoning, robustness, autonomous learning, long-horizon agency, and systematic generalization.
- It is unclear whether these are merely engineering deficiencies or signs that LLMs lack some essential machinery.
- Data, compute, energy, economics, regulation, and diminishing returns could slow progress.
- The eventual winning system may be hybrid enough that calling it “LLM scaling/refinement” becomes questionable.
- AGI by 2035 itself is far from guaranteed.

So my view is: **Gary Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong**, but so is confidence that “just keep scaling LLMs” straightforwardly gets us there. The LLM-descendant path is a serious contender and probably the leading near-term candidate, but I’d still put the full event at only about **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I would interpret “scaling and refining LLM architectures” broadly: larger/more efficient transformer-like foundation models, multimodality, tool use, long-context memory, retrieval, synthetic data, reinforcement learning, agent scaffolding, inference-time search, and similar extensions. If “current paradigm” meant only “make text-only next-token predictors bigger,” my estimate would be much lower, perhaps ~10–15%.

Why I put it around **35%**:

- **The pro-scaling case is strong.** The last several years suggest that scaling plus relatively incremental refinements can unlock qualitatively new capabilities: coding, math, multimodal understanding, tool use, instruction following, and increasingly agentic behavior. There is no obvious theoretical reason transformer-like models could not support very general cognition if trained and scaffolded well enough.
- **The incentives and resources are enormous.** If AGI is achieved before 2035, the leading candidates are likely to come from the currently dominant foundation-model ecosystem, because that is where the capital, talent, compute, infrastructure, and deployment feedback loops are concentrated.
- **Many “missing pieces” may be refinements rather than paradigm shifts.** Persistent memory, planning, self-verification, tool use, program synthesis, multimodal grounding, and long-horizon agency could plausibly be added around LLM-like cores without abandoning the paradigm.

But I would not put the probability above 50% because:

- **Reliability and long-horizon reasoning remain major open problems.** Current systems can be impressive but brittle, hallucination-prone, and poor at robust autonomous execution over extended periods.
- **Data, compute, energy, and economics may bite.** Scaling laws may continue, but useful high-quality data and feasible training budgets are not unlimited.
- **AGI may require better world models, causal reasoning, continual learning, embodiment, or symbolic abstraction** than LLM-style training naturally provides.
- **“AGI” itself is ambiguous.** If one requires a system with broad autonomy, robust scientific creativity, self-directed learning, and high reliability across novel environments, the bar is much higher than benchmark-superhuman chatbot performance.

So my rough decomposition is:

- Probability of AGI before 2035 by any route: maybe **45–50%**.
- Conditional probability that, if it happens by then, it is mostly through LLM/foundation-model scaling and refinement: maybe **65–75%**.

That gives a single best estimate of about **35%**.

So I would not call belief in the current paradigm “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default near-certainty. My best estimate is: **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Interpreting the question as an **all-in probability** that AGI is achieved **before 2035** and that the main technical path is **scaled/refined LLM or foundation-model architectures**, not a clean break to a fundamentally different paradigm.

Why the probability is substantial:

- Scaling has produced broad, general capabilities more reliably than many skeptics expected.
- LLM-family systems can absorb language, code, images, video, tools, APIs, and feedback into a fairly general sequence-modeling framework.
- Many apparent weaknesses—math, coding, factuality, tool use, planning—have shown at least partial improvement through scale, post-training, retrieval, scaffolding, synthetic data, and test-time compute.
- The economic incentive is enormous, and many labs will push this direction hard through 2035.
- If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to be a descendant of today’s foundation-model paradigm than a totally new approach, simply because that is where the investment, talent, infrastructure, and empirical traction currently are.

Why I would not put it much higher:

- Current LLMs still struggle with robust long-horizon autonomy, grounded causal understanding, continual learning, reliable self-correction, and low-error reasoning.
- Benchmarks may overstate real-world generality; brittle systems can look intelligent until placed in open-ended environments.
- Data, compute, energy, and deployment costs may create diminishing returns before AGI-level reliability is reached.
- It remains plausible that genuine AGI needs stronger world models, explicit planning, neurosymbolic reasoning, embodied learning, or other architectural changes that are more than “LLM refinement.”
- “AGI” itself is ambiguous: a digital worker that outperforms humans at most cognitive tasks is easier than a broadly autonomous, physically grounded, scientifically creative general intelligence.

So I would not call belief in the LLM-scaling path “delusional”; it deserves serious probability mass. But I also would not treat it as the default-certain outcome.

**My single best estimate: 35%.**

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I would treat this as a serious possibility, not “delusional,” but also not the default outcome.

Why the probability is substantial:

- Frontier LLM-family systems have improved rapidly through scale, better data, post-training, tool use, long context, multimodality, test-time compute, and RL-style reasoning refinement.
- DeepSeek-style gains suggest that “scaling” is not just bigger pretraining runs; algorithmic and training-efficiency improvements within the LLM paradigm can produce large capability jumps.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, the currently dominant foundation-model ecosystem is the most likely launch point, because it has the talent, capital, infrastructure, data pipelines, and deployment feedback loops.
- Many apparent LLM weaknesses—math, coding, planning, tool use, hallucination—have been improving, sometimes faster than skeptics expected.

Why I would not put it above 50%:

- Current systems still struggle with robust long-horizon autonomy, reliability, grounding, causal reasoning, continual learning, and coherent world models.
- “Can pass hard benchmarks” is still far from “can autonomously replace a broadly competent human across most cognitive work.”
- Data, compute, energy, economics, and regulation could slow progress.
- The systems that eventually reach AGI may include enough new ingredients—explicit world models, search/planning architectures, persistent memory, self-improving agents, neuro-symbolic components, embodied learning—that calling them merely “scaled/refined LLMs” may become misleading.

So my single best estimate is: **35% chance that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily through continued scaling and refinement of LLM/foundation-model architectures.**

Data, compute, energy, economics, and regulation could also slow progress before 2035.

Why not lower: scaling has repeatedly produced broader and more transferable capabilities than many skeptics expected. The LLM/foundation-model paradigm is extraordinarily well-funded, empirically productive, and adaptable: multimodal training, retrieval, tool use, verification, memory, agent loops, and stronger post-training may address many present limitations without requiring a clean paradigm break. If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to emerge from the currently dominant foundation-model lineage than from an undeveloped alternative.

So I would not call expectation of LLM-path AGI “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome. My single best estimate: **35% by 2035**.

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I interpret the question as: *By the start of 2035, will some lab build something reasonably called AGI, and will the main route be scaled/refined LLM-like foundation models rather than a fundamentally different paradigm?*

Why this is a serious possibility:

- LLMs have already shown unusually broad transfer across language, code, math, tool use, multimodal understanding, and interactive tasks.
- Scaling laws, better data curation, synthetic data, reinforcement learning, inference-time search, tool use, memory, and multimodality could plausibly close many gaps without abandoning the paradigm.
- The largest labs are investing overwhelmingly in this direction, so if AGI happens soon, path dependence favors the current paradigm.

Why I would not put it above even odds:

- Current systems still have major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounded understanding, and autonomous self-correction.
- “More scale” may face diminishing returns from data quality, compute cost, energy, chip supply, and evaluation bottlenecks.
- A system that is robustly human-level across domains may require deeper changes: explicit world models, different learning regimes, embodied interaction, neurosymbolic methods, or other architectures not well described as refined LLMs.
- The deadline—before 2035—is close enough that even rapid progress may not suffice.

So my view is: **not delusional, but far from certain. Around a one-in-three chance: 35%.**

Interpreting “AGI” as a system that can perform most economically valuable cognitive work at or above competent human level, and interpreting “LLM path” broadly to include multimodal foundation models, tool use, memory, agents, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and test-time reasoning/search, my best estimate is:

**40%**

Why not higher: current LLM-derived systems still have unresolved weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and robustness under distribution shift. It is also plausible that compute/data/energy limits or diminishing returns make “just scale and refine” insufficient, or that AGI requires a more agentic/world-model-based architecture that would count as a real paradigm shift.

Why not lower: the empirical trend is strong. Scaling plus post-training has repeatedly converted apparent conceptual limitations into engineering problems, and LLMs already serve as unusually general cognitive substrates. Tool use, code execution, retrieval, multimodality, self-play/synthetic data, and test-time compute could plausibly close many remaining gaps without abandoning the foundation-model paradigm. Also, “by any lab or company before 2035” gives many shots on goal.

So I would not call belief in this path “delusional,” but I would also not treat it as the default certainty. **Single best-estimate probability: 40%.**

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I would treat it as far from assured: **about a 30% chance by 2035.**

- Massive investment across multiple labs makes it plausible that the needed refinements arrive through engineering and scale rather than a clean theoretical breakthrough.

So my best estimate is: **35%**.  
Not “near-certain,” but also not “delusional.”

Assuming “AGI” means a system that can reliably perform and learn across most economically valuable cognitive domains at roughly human-or-better level, my single best estimate is:

**35%**

That is an **unconditional** probability that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily via scaling/refining LLM-style foundation models, including multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, synthetic data, RL fine-tuning, agent scaffolding, and inference-time search — but without a fundamentally different paradigm taking over.

Why not higher:

- Current LLMs remain weak at robust long-horizon planning, causal reasoning, truthfulness, continual learning, and autonomous self-correction.
- Scaling may hit data, compute, energy, or economic bottlenecks.
- Some missing ingredients may require deeper architectural changes: grounded world models, model-based RL, neurosymbolic reasoning, embodied learning, or new forms of memory and agency.
- “Looks good on benchmarks” can overstate real general intelligence.

Why not lower:

- The empirical scaling record has been unusually strong.
- Many apparent limitations have softened with scale, tool use, better prompting, RLHF/RLAIF, code training, multimodality, and inference-time compute.
- The LLM paradigm is broad and flexible; future “LLMs” may look like multimodal agentic world models while still being descendants of today’s transformer/foundation-model stack.
- The incentives and investment are enormous, so if this path can work, many labs will push it hard.

So I’d put Gary Marcus-style “near zero / delusional” skepticism too low, but I also would not call LLM-scaling-to-AGI before 2035 the most likely outcome. My best estimate: **35%**.

Assuming “AGI” means a system with broadly human-level competence across most cognitive tasks, including robust autonomy, learning, planning, and real-world usefulness—not just a very strong chatbot—my best estimate is:

**30%**

It is not clear that scaling plus incremental architecture/post-training improvements will solve these in time, and “AGI” is a much higher bar than impressive benchmark performance or economically useful automation.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not make it the odds-on expectation. My single best-estimate probability: **30%**.

1. Something reasonably called **AGI** is reached before 2035 — e.g., broadly human-level or better performance across most cognitive work, with robust learning, reasoning, planning, and autonomy; and

- The last decade’s evidence strongly favors the view that **scaling predictive foundation models produces unexpectedly general capabilities**.
- Many apparent LLM weaknesses — math, coding, planning, tool use, factuality — have improved with scale, scaffolding, retrieval, RL, synthetic data, and inference-time compute.
- If AGI arrives by 2035, it is fairly likely to come from the institutions and methods already closest to frontier capability: large foundation models plus refinements.

Why not higher?

- Current LLMs still struggle with **robust causal reasoning, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, grounded world models, reliability, and out-of-distribution generalization**.
- It is unclear whether next-token/self-supervised prediction plus RL-style refinement is sufficient for genuinely general agency, as opposed to very broad but brittle competence.
- Compute, data, energy, regulation, and diminishing returns may slow progress.
- Future “AGI” may include LLMs but depend crucially on a different core idea: model-based RL, neuro-symbolic reasoning, explicit world-model agents, new memory/learning systems, or something not yet dominant.

- **P(AGI before 2035): ~50%**

**Best-estimate probability: 40%.**

I interpret “LLM paradigm” broadly: transformer/foundation-model descendants scaled in compute/data, with multimodality, long context/memory, tool use, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and test-time reasoning. Not just “a bigger chatbot,” but still recognizably an extension of today’s LLM stack.

**Why it might not:** current systems still have major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon agency, causal/world modeling, grounded learning, self-correction, and out-of-distribution robustness. So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not make it the clear favorite. My single best estimate is **40%** that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily through scaled/refined LLM-style architectures.

Interpreting the question as: **“What is the unconditional probability that AGI is achieved before 2035 and that the main route is scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models rather than a fundamentally different paradigm?”**

**My best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Why not lower:

- The current foundation-model paradigm has produced surprisingly general capabilities from scale, data, post-training, tool use, multimodality, and inference-time reasoning.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is more likely to grow out of the dominant, heavily funded, rapidly iterated paradigm than from a clean-slate alternative.
- “LLM architecture” can plausibly absorb many additions—memory, agents, search, RL, synthetic data, multimodal world models—without ceasing to be recognizably part of the same lineage.

Why not higher:

- Current systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon agency, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounded understanding, and robust out-of-distribution reasoning.
- Scaling laws may hit bottlenecks: data quality, compute cost, energy, latency, regulation, and diminishing returns.
- The eventual successful system may require architectural changes large enough to count as a new paradigm: explicit world models, neuro-symbolic reasoning, embodied RL, planning-centric architectures, or something not yet prominent.
- “AGI” is a high and ambiguous threshold; many impressive AI systems short of robust general intelligence may appear before then.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome. My single-number estimate is:

**35%**.

By this I mean: roughly a **40% chance that a broadly recognized AGI-level system appears before 2035 and is best described as a scaled/refined descendant of today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm**—e.g. larger/more efficient multimodal transformers, better post-training, memory, tool use, retrieval, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, RL-style reasoning training—rather than a clearly new paradigm.

Why that high:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced qualitatively broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- LLM-like architectures are flexible general-purpose learners, not merely “chatbots.”
- Many weaknesses—memory, tool use, planning, multimodality, code execution, self-critique—can plausibly be improved without abandoning the paradigm.
- Enormous compute, capital, talent, and competitive pressure are being directed at exactly this path.

Why not higher:

- Current systems still show brittleness, hallucination, weak causal/world modeling, poor long-horizon autonomy, and unreliable reasoning.
- Data, compute, energy, and economic constraints may slow returns from scaling.
- Human-level generality may require stronger forms of grounded learning, active exploration, continual learning, planning, or symbolic/causal structure than today’s LLM-centered systems provide.
- The boundary between “refinement” and “new paradigm” is fuzzy; if AGI requires major architectural or training-objective changes, I would not count that as simple LLM scaling.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional.” But I also would not treat it as the default with overwhelming probability. My single best guess is **40%**.

rows.answer:
I’d put the probability at **35%**.

By that I mean: **an AGI-level system is achieved before 2035, and the main technical lineage is recognizably scaled/refined LLM or foundation-model architecture — e.g., larger multimodal transformers, better post-training/RL, tool use, retrieval, memory, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, and test-time compute — rather than a fundamentally different AI paradigm.**

Why not lower?

- Current LLMs already show surprisingly broad transfer across language, coding, reasoning, tutoring, planning-ish tasks, and multimodal understanding.
- Scaling laws have been unusually reliable, and “refinements” such as RLHF/RLAIF, chain-of-thought-style training, tool use, retrieval, long context, and agent loops plausibly address many current weaknesses without requiring a full paradigm break.
- The world’s leading labs, talent, compute, and capital are overwhelmingly concentrated around the foundation-model paradigm. If AGI happens soon, it is more likely to emerge from that ecosystem than from an underdeveloped alternative.
- Many supposed “missing ingredients” — memory, planning, search, interaction, grounding, self-improvement — can be bolted onto or integrated with LLM-like systems.

Why not higher?

- Current systems remain brittle: hallucination, weak long-horizon planning, poor causal/world modeling, limited autonomous learning, and unreliable reasoning are not minor defects if the bar is robust AGI.
- Scaling may hit bottlenecks in data quality, compute cost, energy, latency, or diminishing returns.
- It is unclear whether next-token-prediction-derived systems can acquire the kind of grounded, persistent, goal-directed cognition needed for strong AGI.
- “AGI before 2035” itself is uncertain; even if foundation models keep improving rapidly, crossing the threshold from very capable assistant to robust general intelligence may require more than incremental refinement.

So my single best estimate is:

**35% probability that AGI before 2035 is reached primarily through scaling/refining LLM-style architectures.**

So my estimate is: **about a 30% chance that AGI, if achieved before 2035, is achieved primarily through scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.** That makes it a serious possibility, but not the most likely outcome.

rows.answer:
My best-estimate probability: **35%**.

By that I mean: an AGI-level system exists before 2035 and is achieved mainly through the current LLM/foundation-model lineage — larger and better-trained transformer-like models, multimodality, tool use, memory, retrieval, RL/post-training, synthetic data, inference-time reasoning, agent scaffolding — rather than requiring a fundamentally different paradigm.

A rough decomposition:

- **Chance of AGI before 2035:** ~45–50%, depending on definition.
- **Chance that, if it happens by then, it comes mainly from the LLM/foundation-model path:** ~65–75%.
- Combined: roughly **30–40%**, with **35%** as my single best estimate.

Why that high: scaling has continued to produce broad capability gains; LLMs already show surprising generality in language, coding, math, tool use, and multimodal tasks; huge investment is accelerating compute and algorithmic refinement; and many apparent “missing pieces” — memory, planning, reliability, self-correction, tool use, long-horizon agency — may be solvable within the foundation-model paradigm rather than requiring a clean break.

Why not higher: current systems remain brittle, hallucinate, struggle with robust long-horizon planning, have weak causal/world models, lack reliable continual learning, and can fail badly out of distribution. It is plausible that genuine AGI needs deeper architectural changes: explicit world modeling, new forms of search/planning, neurosymbolic reasoning, embodiment, or other cognitive architectures. Compute, data, energy, regulation, and diminishing returns could also slow the current path.

If “LLM scaling” means only “make a bigger next-token predictor,” I’d put the probability much lower, perhaps **10–15%**. But if it includes the broader evolving LLM/foundation-model stack, my estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’d interpret the event as: **before 2035, a system broadly recognizable as AGI is achieved, and the central engine is still an LLM/foundation-model lineage**—scaled, multimodal, post-trained, tool-using, agentic, with memory/search/RL scaffolding—but not a wholly new paradigm where LLMs are incidental.

Why I put it that high:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced real, broad capability gains.
- LLMs are increasingly not “just chatbots”: they can use tools, write code, reason with external computation, process images/audio/video, and serve as general cognitive interfaces.
- If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to come from the heavily funded, rapidly iterated dominant paradigm than from an unproven alternative.
- Many “new” ingredients—retrieval, memory, planning, self-play, synthetic data, verifiers, agents—can plausibly be integrated as refinements of the LLM/foundation-model stack rather than replacing it.

Why I don’t put it above 50%:

- Current models still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, autonomy, and robustness under distribution shift.
- Data, compute, energy, and economic constraints may make simple scaling less productive.
- Benchmarks may overstate generality due to contamination, narrow evaluation, or shallow task formats.
- A genuinely robust AGI may require more explicit world models, planning systems, embodied/interactive learning, or neurosymbolic/model-based architectures—enough to count as a paradigm shift.
- It is also quite possible that AGI is simply not reached before 2035.

A rough decomposition would be: perhaps **~50%** that AGI is reached before 2035 under a moderately broad definition, and **~70%** that, if it is reached that soon, the route is mainly LLM/foundation-model scaling and refinement. Multiplying gives about **35%**.

So I would not call belief in the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default certainty. My single best estimate: **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: ~40%.**

I would interpret this as: *by 2035, some lab reaches broadly human-level, general, autonomous cognitive capability, and the main route is recognizably descended from today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm—scaling, multimodality, tool use, memory, RL/post-training, synthetic data, inference-time search, agents—rather than a clean break such as a fundamentally new symbolic, neuromorphic, embodied-RL, or cognitive-architecture paradigm.*

Why not much lower:

- The empirical trend from scaling transformer-based models has been surprisingly strong.
- LLM-like systems are not “just chatbots” anymore; they can be connected to tools, code execution, search, memory, vision/audio/action spaces, and automated verification.
- Many apparent weaknesses—factuality, planning, long-horizon agency, mathematical reliability—may be mitigated by scaffolding, better training objectives, inference-time compute, and model-generated data.
- If AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to come from the currently dominant, best-funded, fastest-iterating paradigm than from a still-undeveloped alternative.

Why not much higher:

- Current LLMs still lack robust causal/world modeling, persistent autonomous learning, reliable long-horizon planning, and grounded common sense in the strong sense AGI would require.
- Scaling may hit bottlenecks: data quality, compute cost, energy, latency, diminishing returns, and synthetic-data collapse or stagnation.
- “LLM + tools” may produce impressive automation without true general intelligence.
- A system that actually crosses the AGI threshold may require architectural innovations large enough that calling it “LLM scaling/refinement” becomes misleading.
- Safety, regulation, economics, or deployment constraints could slow the path before 2035.

So I would not call Gary Marcus’s skepticism obviously wrong, but “delusional” seems too strong in the other direction. The LLM-centered path is a serious contender, but not a lock.

**My single best estimate: 40%.**

My interpretation: by “AGI” I mean a system that can robustly perform or learn most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly expert-human level, with substantial autonomy; and by “LLM paradigm” I include scaled transformer-like foundation models plus refinements such as multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL/post-training, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, and test-time reasoning—but not a wholly different cognitive/neurosymbolic/embodied paradigm.

- Data, compute, chip, energy, and economic constraints may slow pure scaling.
- Some apparent capability gains may come from benchmark saturation, contamination, or shallow pattern matching rather than robust general intelligence.
- Achieving dependable AGI may require architectural ingredients that are not just “bigger/refined LLMs”—for example, stronger world models, planning systems, embodied learning, neurosymbolic reasoning, or fundamentally different training regimes.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default with high confidence. My single-number estimate is that there is about a **35% chance** that AGI before 2035 is achieved primarily through scaling and refining LLM-like architectures.

Data/compute/economic constraints may bite, and it remains plausible that genuine AGI requires a more fundamental shift—e.g., different learning objectives, architectures, symbolic/causal integration, embodiment, or new forms of memory and planning.

I’d put it at **40%**.

By that I mean: **~40% chance that before 2035, some lab reaches something reasonably called AGI primarily via the current LLM/foundation-model lineage**—scaled transformer-like or successor architectures, multimodal training, tool use, RL/RLAIF, synthetic data, memory, agents, test-time computation, etc.—rather than via a clearly different paradigm such as a primarily symbolic, brain-emulation, neuromorphic, or wholly new cognitive architecture.

- LLMs already show surprising generality across language, code, math, vision, tool use, and instruction following.
- Massive investment, compute growth, synthetic data, agentic scaffolding, and inference-time search could plausibly close many remaining gaps.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, the incumbent paradigm has a big advantage: it is where the talent, infrastructure, data pipelines, and deployment feedback loops are.

Why not higher?

- Current systems still struggle with reliability, long-horizon planning, causal reasoning, persistent world models, autonomous learning, and robust self-correction.
- “Looks smart on benchmarks” may not translate into open-ended competence.
- Data/compute/economic constraints or diminishing returns could bite hard.
- It is plausible that true AGI requires architectural ingredients not well captured by “bigger/refined LLMs,” even if LLMs remain components.

My rough decomposition would be: perhaps **55–60%** chance of AGI by 2035 by any route, conditional on a moderately broad AGI definition, and **~65–70%** chance that if it happens that soon, it is mainly through the LLM/foundation-model path. Multiplying gives roughly **40%**.

So I would not call belief in LLM-path AGI by 2035 “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome. My single best estimate: **40%**.

rows.answer:
My single best-estimate probability: **35%**.

Why not lower: the LLM/foundation-model paradigm has already shown unusually broad transfer, strong scaling behavior, rapid improvement from multimodality, tool use, RL/instruction tuning, code execution, retrieval, memory, and agent scaffolding. Why not higher: current systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, causal/world modeling, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, grounded understanding, and robust planning. It is not clear that simply scaling next-token-prediction-derived architectures, even with clever add-ons, will resolve these. Data, compute, energy, deployment, and safety constraints may also slow progress. And if “AGI” means a system with flexible, autonomous, human-level learning across almost all domains rather than very strong tool-using cognitive automation, the bar is materially higher.

So I would not call belief in this path “delusional,” but I also would not make it the majority forecast. **Best estimate: ~35% chance that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily via scaled/refined LLM-style architectures.**

rows.answer:
**My single best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Assumptions: by **AGI** I mean a system that can autonomously learn, reason, plan, use tools, and perform at or above human level across most economically and intellectually important cognitive tasks, not necessarily one with humanlike consciousness or full robotic embodiment. By “LLM paradigm” I include scaled transformer/foundation-model systems with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, post-training, RL, synthetic data, and inference-time reasoning—so long as the core remains recognizably LLM/foundation-model-centered rather than a wholesale new paradigm.

Why the probability is substantial:

- **Empirical scaling has worked surprisingly well.** Larger models, better data, better post-training, and more inference-time compute have repeatedly produced abilities that many skeptics expected would require different architectures.
- **LLMs are more general than “text predictors” sounds.** Language contains compressed traces of reasoning, planning, social knowledge, coding, science, and tool use. Training on it can produce broad world-model-like competence.
- **The current paradigm is flexible.** Memory, agents, code execution, theorem provers, search, multimodal perception, and reinforcement learning can be bolted onto LLMs without necessarily counting as a paradigm shift.
- **Investment and talent are heavily concentrated here.** If AGI is reachable by continued refinement, the labs pursuing this path have enormous incentives and resources to find it.

Why I do not put the probability higher:

- **Robust autonomy remains unsolved.** Current systems can be impressive yet brittle: hallucination, poor calibration, weak long-horizon planning, inconsistent reasoning, and difficulty maintaining goals over extended tasks are real limitations.
- **Benchmarks may overstate general intelligence.** Passing many tests is not the same as reliable open-ended competence in novel, adversarial, or high-stakes environments.
- **Data and compute may hit diminishing returns.** High-quality human data, energy, chips, and training economics could become limiting, and synthetic data may not fully substitute.
- **Some missing ingredients may be architectural.** Causal reasoning, persistent self-directed learning, grounded world models, agency, and planning may require systems that are more like model-based RL, neurosymbolic reasoning, active learning agents, or something not yet central to today’s LLM stack.
- **“Primarily through LLMs” is itself ambiguous.** If the eventual system uses LLMs plus extensive search, memory, world models, and autonomous experimentation, reasonable people may disagree on whether that counts as scaled/refined LLMs or a new paradigm.

Bottom line: I think Marcus’s “delusional” phrasing is too strong. The LLM-centered route to AGI before 2035 is a serious possibility. But it is far from assured, and the unresolved reliability/agency/generalization problems are large enough that I would put the chance at **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 40%.**

Interpreting the question as: *by before 2035, some lab reaches AGI, and the main technical lineage is scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.*

Why this is plausibly high:

- **LLMs have already shown unusually broad transfer.** They are not just language tools; with code, tool use, multimodality, retrieval, memory, and agent scaffolding, they can approximate many cognitive workflows.
- **The “current paradigm” is elastic.** A future AGI could still be recognizably LLM-descended while using RL, synthetic data, inference-time search, external memory, verifiers, multimodal world models, and tool ecosystems.
- **Scaling has not obviously saturated.** Data quality, compute, algorithmic efficiency, and post-training methods still leave room for large gains.
- **If AGI arrives soon, it is most likely to come from the dominant, best-funded paradigm.** Alternative paradigms exist, but none currently look as commercially and empirically validated as foundation models.

Why it is far from certain:

- **Current systems still lack robust agency.** Long-horizon planning, dependable reasoning, causal understanding, self-correction, and autonomy remain brittle.
- **Benchmarks may exaggerate generality.** LLMs can appear competent while failing under distribution shift, adversarial setups, or tasks requiring grounded models of the world.
- **Compute/data bottlenecks may bite.** More scale may get expensive faster than it gets useful, and high-quality human data is finite.
- **AGI may require architectural ingredients that are not mere refinements**—for example, stronger forms of continual learning, explicit world modeling, planning, or neurosymbolic reasoning.

So I would not call optimism about the LLM/foundation-model route “delusional.” But I also would not treat it as the default outcome. My best estimate is that there is about a **40%** chance that AGI before 2035 is achieved primarily through scaled and refined LLM-like architectures.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’d interpret “LLM paradigm” broadly: transformer/foundation-model systems scaled up and refined with multimodality, tool use, memory, retrieval, agent scaffolding, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and test-time reasoning—not merely “make today’s chatbots bigger.”

Why it could happen:

- The current paradigm has shown unusually broad transfer: language, code, math, tool use, vision, and scientific assistance all improved under scaling plus post-training.
- There is massive investment, and the shortest path to near-term AGI is likely to extend the systems already working.
- Many apparent weaknesses—hallucination, weak planning, poor reliability—may be reducible through better training data, verifiers, tool use, memory, self-play, and inference-time compute rather than a wholly new paradigm.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is somewhat more likely to be from the currently dominant foundation-model line than from a still-undeveloped alternative.

Why I don’t put it much higher:

- Current LLMs still lack robust long-horizon agency, grounded world models, causal reasoning, continual learning, and dependable self-correction.
- Scaling laws may face data, compute, energy, cost, or diminishing-return bottlenecks.
- “AGI” may require architectural changes that are not just refinements: explicit planning, model-based RL, neurosymbolic reasoning, embodied learning, or other systems where LLMs are only components.
- Benchmarks and demos can overstate generality; reliability in open-ended real-world tasks is a harder target than impressive conversation or coding assistance.

My rough decomposition would be: perhaps **~50%** that something plausibly called AGI exists before 2035, and conditional on that, **~70%** that it is reached via an LLM/foundation-model-centered path rather than a fundamentally different paradigm. That yields about **35%**.

So: Marcus’s “delusional” seems too dismissive, but confidence that scaling/refined LLMs alone will get to AGI before 2035 also seems too high.

**35%**

Also, the system that eventually qualifies as AGI may require enough new machinery—e.g. persistent memory, active learning, model-based RL, symbolic planning, embodied interaction, or new cognitive architectures—that calling it merely “scaled/refined LLMs” becomes misleading.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default with high confidence. My best estimate: **35% before 2035**.

I’d put it at **35%**.

Interpretation: by “AGI” I mean a system that can autonomously perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human or better level, robustly across domains. By “LLM architectures” I include scaled multimodal transformer/foundation-model systems with post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, agents, synthetic data, RL, and test-time reasoning—not just a plain chat model.

- Current LLMs still have major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and autonomous task execution.

- The current foundation-model paradigm has produced the fastest broad AI progress so far.
- Scaling, multimodality, tool use, RL/post-training, long context, and agentic scaffolding have repeatedly converted apparent weaknesses into engineering problems.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, there is a strong prior that it will come from the paradigm receiving the overwhelming share of compute, talent, money, and iteration.
- A “paradigm shift” by 2035 may be less likely to mature fast enough than continued refinement of today’s dominant approach.

My rough decomposition would be: perhaps **45–50%** that AGI is reached before 2035 by any route, and **~70% conditional on that** that the main route is recognizably descended from scaled/refined LLM/foundation-model architectures.

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Assumptions: I’m counting “LLM architecture” broadly—large transformer/foundation-model systems, likely multimodal, with tool use, retrieval, memory, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and inference-time search layered around them. I’m not counting a fundamentally new non-LLM cognitive architecture, symbolic reasoning engine, brain-inspired system, or embodied RL paradigm as “LLM scaling/refinement” if it becomes the core driver.

Why the probability is not tiny:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- LLM-derived systems are already strong in language, code, math, tool use, and many professional tasks.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, path dependence favors the current heavily funded paradigm: labs are most likely to get there by extending what already works.
- Some apparent deficiencies—memory, planning, tool use, multimodality, reliability—may be solvable through engineering and training refinements rather than a clean paradigm break.

Why the probability is not higher:

- “AGI before 2035” itself is uncertain.
- Current LLMs remain brittle on long-horizon planning, causal reasoning, autonomous learning, robustness, and truthful self-correction.
- Data, compute, energy, and economic constraints may slow pure scaling.
- The decisive missing ingredient may turn out to be active world-model learning, model-based RL, neurosymbolic reasoning, or some other architecture not well described as scaled LLMs.

So I’d treat Gary Marcus’s “delusional” wording as too strong: the LLM-scaling path to AGI by 2035 is a serious live possibility. But I’d still put it as a substantial minority outcome rather than the default.

**Single-number estimate: 30%.**

rows.answer:
My best estimate: **35%**.

Assumptions: by “AGI” I mean a system that can autonomously perform or learn most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level or better; and by “scaling/refining LLM architectures” I include transformer-like multimodal foundation models with better post-training, RL, tool use, memory, retrieval, synthetic data, agent scaffolding, and inference-time search—not just “make GPT bigger.”

Why not much lower: current LLM-based systems already show broad, surprising transfer; scaling and post-training have repeatedly converted “impossible” tasks into benchmark wins; and if AGI arrives before 2035, the most likely route is probably the heavily funded, empirically successful paradigm already in motion.

Why not much higher: robust autonomy, long-horizon planning, grounding, causal reasoning, continual learning, and reliability remain real gaps; compute/data/economic constraints may bite; and what ultimately works may require architectures closer to active world-modeling, model-based RL, neuro-symbolic systems, or something else sufficiently different from today’s LLM paradigm.

A rough decomposition would be: maybe **45–50%** chance of AGI before 2035 at all, and conditional on that, perhaps **65–75%** chance it is reached primarily through the LLM/foundation-model lineage. Multiplying those gives about one-in-three.

So my single best-estimate probability is: **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I would not call the view “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome.

I’m interpreting the question as: by 2035, a system reaches broadly human-level or superhuman general cognitive competence across most economically relevant intellectual tasks, and the main technical lineage is scaled/refined LLM-style foundation models — including multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, agents, RL/post-training, synthetic data, mixture-of-experts, and inference-time reasoning — rather than a clean break to a fundamentally different paradigm.

Why the probability is substantial:

- The current paradigm has already produced unusually broad transfer: language, code, math, vision-language, tool use, and interactive assistance.
- Scaling plus post-training has repeatedly converted “obvious limitations” into partial strengths faster than many skeptics expected.
- LLM-like systems are a natural substrate for agents: they can use tools, write code, call search, coordinate workflows, and improve through feedback.
- Labs have enormous economic incentives and infrastructure momentum behind this path.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is more likely to come from the dominant high-investment paradigm than from a still-hypothetical replacement.

Why I keep the estimate well below even odds:

- Current systems remain unreliable in ways that matter: hallucination, brittle reasoning, weak long-horizon planning, poor self-monitoring, and inconsistent causal understanding.
- “More scale” may face diminishing returns from data, compute, energy, cost, and synthetic-data quality limits.
- Human-level generality may require better continual learning, world models, embodiment/interaction, planning, or memory architectures than LLM refinement alone naturally provides.
- Benchmarks and demos may overstate progress because they often reward pattern completion rather than robust autonomous competence.
- Some crucial missing ingredient could come from a paradigm shift: neuro-symbolic systems, model-based RL, active world-model learning, brain-inspired architectures, or something not yet prominent.

So my single best estimate is: **35%** that AGI before 2035 is reached primarily through scaled/refined LLM-family architectures rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.

My best estimate: **35%**.

I interpret this as: by **2035**, some lab reaches a broadly human-level, general-purpose AI system mainly by extending today’s foundation-model/LLM paradigm—larger/more efficient multimodal models, better post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, inference-time reasoning, agents—rather than by a clearly different paradigm.

Why it is plausibly that high:

- Scaling has repeatedly produced real capability gains, including coding, math, multimodal understanding, tool use, and long-context reasoning.
- “LLM architecture” is likely to keep absorbing adjacent techniques: RL, search, memory, self-play, synthetic data, robotics data, and agentic scaffolding.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is likely to come from the currently dominant, massively funded path rather than from a clean-slate alternative.

Why it is not higher:

- Current systems still show major weaknesses in reliability, causal reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and autonomous self-correction.
- Data, compute, energy, economics, and regulatory constraints may slow pure scaling.
- Some missing ingredients may require deeper architectural changes: persistent world models, active learning, neurosymbolic reasoning, embodied interaction, or other approaches not well described as “refined LLMs.”
- Benchmarks may overstate progress toward robust general intelligence.

So I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
My single best-estimate probability: **35%**.

By that I mean: about a **35% chance that before 2035, some lab reaches something reasonably describable as AGI primarily via an LLM-descended foundation-model paradigm** — scaled multimodal transformers, better post-training/RL, tool use, memory, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, test-time compute, etc. I would not count a route that depends mainly on a fundamentally new symbolic, neuromorphic, brain-emulation, or non-LLM cognitive architecture.

Why I put it that high:

- Scaling and refinement have repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- LLM-like systems are already unusually general compared with prior AI systems.
- Tool use, code execution, multimodality, memory, and reinforcement learning could compensate for many “pure language model” weaknesses.
- The dominant investment, talent, and infrastructure are concentrated around this paradigm, so if AGI arrives soon, it is likely to be LLM-descended.

Why I do not put it higher:

- Current systems still struggle with reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, causal reasoning, continual learning, and autonomous self-correction.
- Scaling may face data, compute, cost, and energy bottlenecks.
- Some missing pieces may require more than incremental refinement — e.g. new architectures for persistent agency, world modeling, or robust reasoning.
- “AGI” is a high bar if defined as broadly human-level competence across unfamiliar domains, not just impressive benchmark performance.

So I’d say Gary Marcus’s strong dismissal is overconfident, but so is the view that the current LLM paradigm is almost certain to get us to AGI soon. My best estimate is: **35% before 2035 via primarily LLM-style scaling/refinement.**

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I’m interpreting “AGI” as roughly: a system that can autonomously learn and perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at competent human level or better, across domains, without bespoke task-specific engineering. I’m also counting as “LLM scaling/refinement” things like multimodal foundation models, larger/cheaper compute, better post-training/RL, tool use, retrieval, memory, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, and inference-time reasoning—so long as the core remains recognizably descended from today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm.

Why the probability is not tiny:

- LLMs have already shown surprising generality: language, code, math, summarization, tutoring, planning-like behavior, tool use, multimodal perception.
- Many apparent weaknesses may be addressable within the paradigm: hallucination via verification/tools, memory via retrieval, reasoning via inference-time compute and RL, grounding via multimodal/robotic data.
- Huge capital, talent, and hardware investment is concentrated on this paradigm, so if AGI arrives before 2035, it is quite likely to be LLM-centered.
- Scaling laws and algorithmic efficiency gains have not obviously exhausted themselves, even if naive pretraining-only scaling is becoming less sufficient.

Why the probability is not higher:

- Current systems remain brittle on reliability, long-horizon autonomy, causal understanding, continual learning, and robust out-of-distribution reasoning.
- Data, compute, energy, chip supply, and economic constraints could cause diminishing returns.
- “Agentic LLMs” may still fail at the kind of persistent, self-correcting, world-model-based cognition required for AGI.
- A real breakthrough may require something more fundamentally different: richer world models, neuro-symbolic reasoning, embodied learning, new memory/learning architectures, or non-LLM-centered RL systems.
- The definition of AGI matters a lot; under a stricter “can replace almost all human intellectual labor with high reliability” definition, I’d put the number lower.

A rough decomposition would be: perhaps **45–55%** that something reasonably called AGI is reached before 2035 by any route, and conditional on that, maybe **60–70%** that it is primarily through an evolved LLM/foundation-model paradigm. Multiplying those gives a point estimate around **35%**.

So my single best estimate is: **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

My estimate assumes **AGI** means a software system that can reliably learn and perform most economically and intellectually valuable cognitive tasks at or above strong human level, with substantial autonomy—not necessarily consciousness or human-like embodiment. I also interpret “scaling and refining LLM architectures” broadly: transformer-like/multimodal foundation models plus better post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, RL, and test-time reasoning—but not a wholly new symbolic/cognitive/brain-inspired paradigm.

Why not lower:

- LLM-family systems have shown unusually broad transfer across language, code, math, vision, planning-adjacent tasks, and tool use.
- Scaling laws, post-training, inference-time compute, and multimodal training still plausibly have room to run.
- The dominant labs, capital, talent, infrastructure, and feedback loops are all concentrated around this paradigm.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it seems more likely to be an evolved foundation-model agent than a completely separate approach that starts from behind.

Why not higher:

- Current systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon autonomy, causal/world modeling, continual learning, grounding, and robust reasoning.
- Scaling may face data, compute, energy, and economic bottlenecks or diminishing returns.
- “AGI” may require architectural ingredients not naturally obtained by next-token/multimodal prediction plus scaffolding.
- A system that looks impressive in benchmarks may still fall short of robust general intelligence in open-ended real-world deployment.

A rough decomposition would be: perhaps **45%** chance of AGI by 2035 under this definition, and conditional on that, maybe **70–80%** chance it is primarily LLM/foundation-model-derived rather than a fundamentally different paradigm. Multiplying those gives roughly **35%**.

So I would not call belief in the current paradigm “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default-certainty path to AGI. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

I would not call the LLM-scaling path “delusional”; it is a serious contender. But I also would not make it the default outcome.

My rough decomposition:

- **Chance of AGI before 2035 by any route:** ~45–50%, depending heavily on how strict the AGI definition is.
- **Conditional chance that, if it happens by then, it is primarily LLM-descended rather than a fundamentally new paradigm:** ~65–75%.

Multiplying those gives a ballpark around **30–40%**, so my single best estimate is:

> **35% that AGI before 2035 is reached primarily through scaling/refining LLM-style architectures.**

Reasons pushing the estimate up: scaling has worked surprisingly well; LLMs are becoming multimodal, tool-using, agentic, and trainable with feedback/verifiers; most capital and talent are flowing into this paradigm; and “LLM plus memory, tools, RL, synthetic data, and inference-time search” may be enough for broad practical intelligence.

Reasons pushing it down: current systems still have major weaknesses in reliability, long-horizon planning, causal understanding, continual learning, and grounded world modeling; data/compute scaling may hit diminishing returns; and the eventual winning system may require qualitatively different ingredients—e.g. stronger world models, neurosymbolic reasoning, embodied learning, or new forms of RL—not just bigger/refined language models.

So: **plausible, maybe the leading single path, but far from assured — about one chance in three.**

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

By “AGI,” I’m assuming roughly: a system that can perform or learn most economically valuable cognitive tasks at human level or better, with robust transfer, planning, tool use, and autonomy—not necessarily consciousness or human-like embodiment. By “LLM paradigm,” I include transformer-like/multimodal foundation models plus scaling, post-training, RL, tool use, retrieval, memory, synthetic data, agents, and inference-time reasoning—so long as the core remains recognizably descended from today’s LLM/foundation-model approach.

Why I’d put the probability this high:

- Scaling has worked surprisingly well so far, across language, code, vision, math, and tool-use-adjacent tasks.
- LLMs appear to learn broad world and task representations from prediction objectives more effectively than many skeptics expected.
- The investment level is enormous; by 2035, labs may have much larger compute budgets, better data pipelines, better synthetic data, and more efficient architectures.
- Many apparent weaknesses—memory, hallucination, tool use, planning, verification—may be mitigated by engineering refinements rather than a wholesale new paradigm.
- If AGI happens soon, it is more likely to come from the currently dominant research trajectory than from a clean-sheet alternative that has not yet demonstrated comparable scaling behavior.

Why I would not put it above 50%:

- Current models remain brittle: they hallucinate, fail at long-horizon planning, lack stable agency, and struggle with causal/physical grounding.
- “Looks intelligent on benchmarks” may not scale into robust general competence under distribution shift.
- Data, compute, energy, and economic constraints may slow the scaling path.
- AGI may require deeper advances in continual learning, world modeling, embodiment, symbolic abstraction, planning, or causal reasoning that are not just “bigger/better LLMs.”
- The deadline—before 2035—is relatively soon for solving reliability and autonomy at AGI level.

So my answer is: **35% that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily through scaled/refined LLM-style architectures rather than a fundamentally different paradigm.** Not “delusional,” but also far from assured.

rows.answer:
**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Assumptions: by “AGI” I mean a software system that can autonomously learn and perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human-expert level or better. By “LLM path” I include transformer/foundation-model systems with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL/post-training, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, and test-time reasoning—so long as the core approach is still recognizably scaling/refining LLM-like architectures, not a wholly different paradigm.

Why the probability is substantial:

- **Empirical scaling has kept paying off.** Larger models, better data, better post-training, and more inference-time compute have repeatedly produced capabilities skeptics expected to require more explicit reasoning machinery.
- **LLMs are becoming general interfaces to tools.** Coding, search, symbolic solvers, simulators, memory systems, and external verifiers can compensate for many native weaknesses.
- **If AGI arrives before 2035, the incumbent paradigm has a major advantage.** The money, talent, infrastructure, data pipelines, and deployment feedback loops are overwhelmingly concentrated around foundation-model systems.
- **“Refinement” is a broad category.** Multimodal world models, long-context memory, agent training, self-play/synthetic curricula, and test-time planning could plausibly be enough without a clean paradigm break.

Why it is not higher:

- **Reliability and long-horizon autonomy remain unresolved.** Current systems can be impressive yet brittle, especially on tasks requiring sustained planning, verification, causal understanding, and adaptation under distribution shift.
- **Data/compute economics may bite.** Scaling may continue but with diminishing returns, bottlenecks in high-quality data, energy, chips, and inference costs.
- **AGI may require deeper architectural changes.** Continual learning, grounded world modeling, robust agency, and explicit reasoning may need systems that are no longer fairly described as “refined LLMs.”
- **“AGI before 2035” itself is uncertain.** Even if LLMs are the leading near-term route, the deadline is demanding.

So my single best estimate is: **35%** that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily via scaling and refining LLM-like architectures, rather than via a fundamentally different paradigm.

I would put the chance at **about 40%** that AGI, if achieved before 2035, is reached primarily through the current LLM/foundation-model trajectory: larger and better multimodal transformer-like models, improved post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, agent scaffolding, synthetic data, inference-time search, and related refinements.

Interpreting “AGI” as broadly human-level performance across most economically relevant cognitive tasks, and “LLM paradigm” as transformer/foundation-model descendants with multimodality, tool use, memory, agents, RL/post-training, synthetic data, and inference-time reasoning—but not a wholesale new cognitive architecture—my best estimate is:

Why not higher: today’s systems still have deep issues with robustness, causal understanding, long-horizon agency, autonomous learning, grounded world models, and dependable reasoning. Compute, data, regulation, safety constraints, and economic diminishing returns could also slow the LLM route before it reaches true AGI.

So I would not call belief in the LLM-scaling/refinement path “delusional,” but I also would not make it the majority bet. My single best-estimate probability: **35%**.

Interpreting **AGI** as a system with broadly human-level competence across most cognitive tasks, able to learn new domains, use tools, plan over long horizons, and operate reliably enough to replace or outperform humans in many knowledge-work roles, my best estimate is:

**35%**

That is: I’d assign roughly a **35% probability** that AGI is reached before 2035 primarily via scaled and refined LLM/foundation-model architectures, rather than via a clearly different paradigm.

Why not lower:

- The empirical progress from scaling, post-training, tool use, multimodality, coding ability, reasoning-time compute, synthetic data, and agent scaffolding has been substantial.
- The current frontier paradigm is attracting enormous talent, capital, compute, and infrastructure.
- “LLM architecture” is already expanding beyond text: multimodal transformers, retrieval, memory, tool use, RL, long-context reasoning, and agentic systems may be enough to close many apparent gaps without a clean paradigm break.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it is plausibly more likely to come from the dominant existing research program than from an undeveloped alternative.

Why not higher:

- Current systems still have serious weaknesses in reliability, causal understanding, long-horizon autonomy, robust planning, continual learning, and grounded world modeling.
- Some failures may not be solved merely by scale; they may require more explicit search, symbolic reasoning, embodied learning, new memory systems, or different training objectives.
- Data, compute, energy, economics, and regulation may slow progress.
- Benchmarks can overstate real-world generality, and “AGI” is a high bar if defined as dependable, autonomous, broadly adaptive intelligence rather than impressive demo performance.

So I would not call belief in LLM-driven AGI by 2035 “delusional,” but I also would not treat it as the default outcome.

**Single best-estimate probability: 35%.**

Compute/data limits and diminishing returns may bite. It is also possible that “AGI” requires a more explicit integration of reasoning, memory, search, embodiment, or symbolic/causal structure—enough that calling it merely “scaled/refined LLMs” would be misleading.

So my single best estimate is: **35%**.
